definition: Shameless audacity; supreme self-confidence bordering on arrogance.
exampleUsage: It takes real chutzpah to ask for a raise on your first day.
etymology: From Hebrew ḥuṣpā, meaning insolence or audacity.

definition: A foolish or contemptible person.
exampleUsage: Don't be such a schmuck — apologize already.
etymology: From Yiddish shmok, originally meaning a fool or a penis.

definition: To offer unwanted advice or commentary, especially as a spectator.
exampleUsage: Stop kibitzing and let me play my own hand!
etymology: From Yiddish kibiṭsn, from German Kiebitz (lapwing, a noisy bird).

definition: A clumsy, awkward person.
exampleUsage: He's such a klutz — he tripped over his own feet again.
etymology: From Yiddish klots, meaning a wooden block or beam.

definition: To eat snacks or food between meals; also the snack itself.
exampleUsage: Let's nosh on some bagels before the meeting.
etymology: From Yiddish nashn, meaning to nibble or eat sweets.

definition: To carry or drag something heavy or cumbersome; a tedious journey.
exampleUsage: I had to schlep all these groceries up five flights of stairs.
etymology: From Yiddish shlepn, meaning to drag or pull.

definition: A person of integrity and honor; a genuinely good human being.
exampleUsage: He volunteered every weekend — a real mensch.
etymology: From Yiddish mentsh, meaning a person, from German Mensch.

definition: To complain persistently and annoyingly; also the complainer.
exampleUsage: She kvetches about everything — the weather, the food, the traffic.
etymology: From Yiddish kvetshn, meaning to squeeze or press.

definition: A comedian's routine or gimmick; a person's characteristic behavior.
exampleUsage: His whole shtick is playing the clueless tourist.
etymology: From Yiddish shtik, meaning a piece or a prank.

definition: An expert or connoisseur in a particular field.
exampleUsage: Ask Rachel — she's the maven when it comes to wine.
etymology: From Yiddish meyvn, from Hebrew mēvīn, meaning one who understands.

definition: To burst or collapse from strong emotion, especially excitement or laughter.
exampleUsage: When she heard the news, she nearly plotzed.
etymology: From Yiddish platsn, meaning to burst or crack.

definition: Hopelessly lost; completely confused or disoriented.
exampleUsage: We got completely farblondjet trying to find the restaurant.
etymology: From Yiddish farblon(d)zhet, meaning having gone astray.

definition: A chronically unlucky person; someone for whom nothing goes right.
exampleUsage: The schlemiel spills the soup; the shlimazl gets it spilled on him.
etymology: From Yiddish shlimmazl, from Middle High German slim (bad) + Hebrew mazzāl (luck).

definition: A thief or dishonest person; a scoundrel.
exampleUsage: That gonif charged me double for a simple repair.
etymology: From Yiddish ganev, from Hebrew gannāv, meaning thief.

definition: Craziness; senseless behavior or absurd goings-on.
exampleUsage: Enough with this mishegoss — just pick a restaurant already!
etymology: From Yiddish meshugaas, from Hebrew meshugga'ath, meaning madness.

definition: Overwhelmed with emotion; choked up and unable to speak.
exampleUsage: She got all farklempt during her daughter's wedding speech.
etymology: From Yiddish farklempt, meaning squeezed or pressed.

definition: A small decorative trinket or knickknack of little value.
exampleUsage: Her shelves are covered with tchotchkes from every vacation.
etymology: From Yiddish tshatshke, possibly from a Slavic diminutive.

definition: Excessive sentimentality; also rendered chicken fat used in cooking.
exampleUsage: The movie was pure schmaltz — everyone was crying by the end.
etymology: From Yiddish shmalts, meaning rendered fat.

definition: A persistently dull, boring, or annoying person; a pest.
exampleUsage: That nudnik called me five times today about the same thing.
etymology: From Yiddish nudnik, from Russian nudnyj, meaning tiresome.

definition: Having a full, rounded figure; plump in an attractive way.
exampleUsage: The painting featured a zaftig woman reclining on a couch.
etymology: From Yiddish zaftik, meaning juicy, from German saftig.

definition: Grandmother; an affectionate term for one's grandma.
exampleUsage: My bubbe makes the best chicken soup you've ever tasted.
etymology: From Yiddish bobe, a familiar term for grandmother, likely from a Slavic root.

definition: To chat in a friendly, intimate way; to network or make small talk.
exampleUsage: He spent the whole party schmoozing with the executives.
etymology: From Yiddish shmuesn, meaning to chat, from Hebrew shmu'oth (rumors, tales).

definition: An exclamation of dismay, grief, pain, or exasperation.
exampleUsage: Oy, I can't believe I left my keys in the car again!
etymology: A natural Yiddish interjection, possibly related to German and Slavic exclamations of distress.

definition: A minor malfunction, snag, or technical problem.
exampleUsage: There was a glitch in the system that caused all the emails to bounce.
etymology: From Yiddish glitsh, meaning a slippery place or a slip, from glitshn (to slide).

definition: A lengthy or extravagant speech or sales pitch.
exampleUsage: The salesman gave us his whole spiel about the warranty.
etymology: From Yiddish shpil, meaning play or game, from German Spiel.

definition: To feel intense pride and joy, especially about someone else's accomplishment.
exampleUsage: She was kvelling when her son got accepted to medical school.
etymology: From Yiddish kveln, meaning to well up with pride, from German quellen (to gush).

definition: Trash; worthless junk; something of very low quality.
exampleUsage: That movie was absolute dreck — don't waste your time.
etymology: From Yiddish drek, meaning filth or excrement, from German Dreck.

definition: The buttocks; the rear end.
exampleUsage: Sit your tuchus down and finish your homework.
etymology: From Yiddish tokhes, from Hebrew taḥath, meaning under or beneath.

definition: A dense, ring-shaped bread roll that is boiled before baking.
exampleUsage: I'll take a bagel with cream cheese and lox, please.
etymology: From Yiddish beygl, possibly from Middle High German böugel (ring or bracelet).

definition: Having chutzpah; nervy, brazen, or impudent.
exampleUsage: That was a very chutzpadik thing to say to your boss.
etymology: From Yiddish khutspadik, the adjectival form of khutspe (chutzpah) with the suffix -dik.

definition: A member of a kibbutz; someone who lives on a communal settlement in Israel.
exampleUsage: My uncle was a kibbutznik for twenty years before moving to New York.
etymology: From Hebrew kibbutz (gathering) with the Yiddish/Slavic agent suffix -nik.

definition: Face, especially a cute or sweet face.
exampleUsage: Look at that shayna punim — what a beautiful baby!
etymology: From Yiddish ponim, from Hebrew pānīm, meaning face.

definition: A beggar or freeloader; someone who habitually cadges from others.
exampleUsage: That schnorrer always shows up at dinner but never brings anything.
etymology: From Yiddish shnorer, from German schnorren (to beg).

definition: A big shot; an important or influential person, often a wheeler-dealer.
exampleUsage: He thinks he's a real macher, but nobody takes him seriously.
etymology: From Yiddish makher, meaning maker or doer, from German machen (to make).

definition: Stuffed intestine; a traditional sausage-like dish. Also used figuratively for guts or intestines.
exampleUsage: The deli still makes their kishka the old-fashioned way.
etymology: From Yiddish kishke, meaning intestine, from a Slavic root.

definition: A long, tedious, or complicated story or account. Literally, a scroll.
exampleUsage: She told me the whole megillah about her trip — every single detail.
etymology: From Hebrew megillāh, meaning scroll, referring to the Book of Esther read on Purim.

definition: The entire extended family; the whole clan.
exampleUsage: The whole mishpocha came together for Passover seder.
etymology: From Hebrew mishpāḥāh, meaning family or clan.

definition: A habitual bungler; a clumsy, awkward, unlucky person.
exampleUsage: The schlemiel spills his soup; the shlimazl is the one he spills it on.
etymology: From Yiddish shlemil, possibly from the name Shelumiel in the Book of Numbers.

definition: An exemplary homemaker; a capable, efficient woman who runs her household admirably.
exampleUsage: She's a real baleboste — the house is spotless and dinner is always on time.
etymology: From Yiddish balebuste, the feminine of balebos (master of the house), from Hebrew ba'al (master) + bayit (house).

definition: A pitifully meek or timid person; a hapless nobody.
exampleUsage: Poor nebbish — he couldn't even stand up for himself at the meeting.
etymology: From Yiddish nebekh or nebakh, an exclamation of pity, from Czech or Polish roots.

definition: Money. Also refers to chocolate coins given during Hanukkah.
exampleUsage: The kids got a bag of Hanukkah gelt after spinning the dreidel.
etymology: From Yiddish gelt, meaning money, from German Geld.

definition: To sweat; also a steam bath or sauna.
exampleUsage: I was schvitzing so much in that suit I thought I'd pass out.
etymology: From Yiddish shvitsn, meaning to sweat, from German schwitzen.

definition: Embittered; sullen and mean-spirited; having a permanently sour disposition.
exampleUsage: Don't mind her — she's been farbissen ever since the divorce.
etymology: From Yiddish farbisn, meaning having bitten into bitterness, from baysn (to bite).

definition: A person who gossips excessively; a busybody.
exampleUsage: Don't tell her anything — she's the biggest yenta on the block.
etymology: From the Yiddish personal name Yente, popularized as a gossip by characters in Yiddish theater.

definition: Pride and joy, especially from the achievements of one's children.
exampleUsage: Her parents shepped naches when she graduated top of her class.
etymology: From Yiddish nakhes, from Hebrew naḥath, meaning contentment or satisfaction.

definition: A crotchety, fussy old man. A vulgar but common insult for an elderly person who is out of touch.
exampleUsage: That alter kocker still thinks a nickel is a lot of money.
etymology: From Yiddish alter (old) + kaker (defecator), a coarse but widely used expression.

definition: An outcast; a scoundrel or worthless person thrown out by society.
exampleUsage: They treated him like an oysvorf after the scandal.
etymology: From Yiddish oysvorf, meaning something thrown out, from oys (out) + vorfn (to throw).

definition: Destiny or fate; often refers to one's predestined soulmate.
exampleUsage: When they met, they both knew it was bashert.
etymology: From Yiddish bashert, meaning destined or fated, from German beschert (bestowed).

definition: Smelly, lousy, rotten; used as a strong term of contempt.
exampleUsage: Get that farshtinkener cat off the kitchen table!
etymology: From Yiddish farshtunken, meaning stinking, from German Gestank (stench).

definition: A young, inexperienced, or insignificant person; literally a bed-wetter.
exampleUsage: What does that little pisher know about running a business?
etymology: From Yiddish pisher, from pishn (to urinate), used to belittle someone as immature.

definition: A shame or disgrace, especially one that reflects on one's family.
exampleUsage: It's a shande the way he treats his elderly mother.
etymology: From Yiddish shande, meaning shame or disgrace, from German Schande.

definition: Trouble, worries, or aggravation; heartache and woe.
exampleUsage: Between the lawsuit and the flood, we've had nothing but tsuris.
etymology: From Yiddish tsores, from Hebrew ṣārōth, meaning troubles or distress.

definition: An impractical dreamer with no business sense; literally an air person.
exampleUsage: He has a hundred ideas and no income — a total luftmensch.
etymology: From Yiddish luft (air) + mentsh (person), describing someone with their head in the clouds.

definition: Nothing; something trivially small or worthless.
exampleUsage: I worked all weekend and they paid me bubkes.
etymology: From Yiddish bobkes, literally meaning goat droppings, used to mean something worthless.

definition: Warm, cozy, and unpretentious; having a homey, welcoming quality.
exampleUsage: The restaurant had a haimish feel — like eating at your grandmother's house.
etymology: From Yiddish heymish, meaning homelike, from heym (home), from German Heim.

definition: Mixed up, confused, or discombobulated.
exampleUsage: After the time change, my whole schedule is completely farfufket.
etymology: From Yiddish farfufket, an expressive intensified form using the far- prefix indicating excess.

definition: A matzo ball; a dumpling made from matzo meal traditionally served in soup.
exampleUsage: Bubbe's knaydlach are so fluffy they practically float out of the bowl.
etymology: From Yiddish kneydl, meaning dumpling, from German Knödel.

definition: To push or shove; also a vulgar term for sexual intercourse.
exampleUsage: Don't shtup me — I'm getting on the bus as fast as I can.
etymology: From Yiddish shtupn, meaning to push or stuff, from German stupsen.

definition: Not kosher; forbidden by Jewish dietary law. Figuratively, anything forbidden or suspect.
exampleUsage: You can't serve shrimp at the reception — it's treyf!
etymology: From Yiddish treyf, from Hebrew terefah, meaning torn or unfit for consumption.

definition: Excessively decorated or overly done; cluttered, gaudy, or slapped together.
exampleUsage: Her living room is so ongepotchket you can barely find a place to sit.
etymology: From Yiddish ongepotchket, from potchken (to fuss or tinker), meaning overworked or messed up.

definition: A strong alcoholic drink, especially whiskey or brandy.
exampleUsage: Grandpa always had a little schnapps after Friday night dinner.
etymology: From Yiddish shnapps, from German Schnaps, meaning a dram of liquor.

definition: Cured or smoked salmon, traditionally served on a bagel.
exampleUsage: No Sunday brunch is complete without lox and bagels.
etymology: From Yiddish laks, from German Lachs, meaning salmon.

definition: Head; often used figuratively to mean brains or intelligence.
exampleUsage: Use your kopf — the answer is obvious!
etymology: From Yiddish kop, from German Kopf, meaning head.

definition: A house of shame; a disreputable place or a brothel.
exampleUsage: They called that run-down hotel a regular shandhoyz.
etymology: From Yiddish shand (shame) + hoyz (house), a compound meaning a disgraceful establishment.

definition: A joyous gathering or celebration, often with singing, stories, and toasts.
exampleUsage: The Rebbe held a farbrengen that lasted well past midnight.
etymology: From Yiddish farbrengen, meaning to spend time together or celebrate.

definition: A chilled soup made from sorrel leaves, typically served with sour cream.
exampleUsage: In the summer, Bubbe always made cold schav with a dollop of sour cream.
etymology: From Yiddish shtshav, from Polish szczaw, meaning sorrel.

definition: A communal settlement in Israel, originally agricultural, based on collective ownership.
exampleUsage: She spent a summer volunteering on a kibbutz near the Sea of Galilee.
etymology: From Hebrew qibbutz, meaning gathering or collective.

definition: A Jewish prayer of mourning recited for the dead; also refers to the mourner.
exampleUsage: He said kaddish for his father every day for a year.
etymology: From Aramaic qaddish, meaning holy or sanctified.

definition: A sharp blow or hit; a punch or smack.
exampleUsage: He gave the stuck jar lid a good zetz and it popped right open.
etymology: From Yiddish zets, meaning a smack or blow.

definition: A fellow countryman; someone from the same town or region in the old country.
exampleUsage: When they discovered they were both from Minsk, they embraced like long-lost landsmen.
etymology: From Yiddish lantsman, from German Landsmann, meaning compatriot.

definition: Common sense; good judgment; practical intelligence.
exampleUsage: That boy has no seychel — he left his coat at school again in January.
etymology: From Yiddish seykhl, from Hebrew sekhel, meaning understanding or intellect.

definition: Nervous energy; restless anxiety; ants in one's pants.
exampleUsage: I've got such shpilkes waiting for the test results.
etymology: From Yiddish shpilkes, meaning pins or needles, from Polish szpilki.

definition: Stinking; smelly; rotten. Used literally or as a strong term of contempt.
exampleUsage: Open a window — something in this refrigerator is farshtunken.
etymology: From Yiddish farshtunkn, past participle of farshtinkn, meaning to stink up.

definition: A foolish, contemptible, or nonsensical person; an idiot.
exampleUsage: Don't listen to that shmegegge — he doesn't know what he's talking about.
etymology: Origin uncertain; possibly a humorous Yiddish coinage with no clear root.

definition: A nasty, contemptible, or vile person.
exampleUsage: Only a real paskudnyak would cheat his own brother.
etymology: From Yiddish paskudnyak, from Polish paskudny, meaning nasty or repulsive.

definition: A slovenly, unkempt person; to dress or carry oneself in a sloppy manner.
exampleUsage: You're going to the interview looking like a shlump? At least tuck in your shirt.
etymology: From Yiddish shlump, related to German schlump, meaning a slovenly person.

definition: Wisdom; also used ironically to mean a clever trick or wisecrack.
exampleUsage: Save your khokhmas for someone who's impressed.
etymology: From Yiddish khokhme, from Hebrew hokhmah, meaning wisdom.

definition: A sweet stew of carrots and fruit; also means a big fuss or commotion over nothing.
exampleUsage: Don't make a whole tsimmes out of it — it's just a small scratch on the car.
etymology: From Yiddish tsimes, possibly from tsu (to) + imes (eat), or from Middle High German.

definition: A big shot; someone who acts important or puts on airs.
exampleUsage: Look at him strutting around like a big knacker with his fancy new car.
etymology: From Yiddish knaker, meaning one who cracks or snaps, suggesting someone who snaps their fingers to summon others.

definition: A thin rolled pancake or crepe filled with cheese, fruit, or other filling.
exampleUsage: For brunch she made cheese blintzes with strawberry sauce.
etymology: From Yiddish blintse, from Ukrainian blynytsya, a diminutive of blyn (pancake).

definition: In Jewish folklore, the malicious spirit of a dead person that possesses the body of a living person.
exampleUsage: The famous play 'The Dybbuk' tells of a young bride possessed by her dead lover's spirit.
etymology: From Yiddish dibek, from Hebrew dibbuk, meaning attachment or clinging.

definition: A small Jewish town or village in Eastern Europe before the Holocaust.
exampleUsage: My great-grandparents grew up in a shtetl in Poland before emigrating.
etymology: From Yiddish shtetl, diminutive of shtot (city), meaning a small town.

definition: In Jewish legend, an animated being made from clay or mud, brought to life through mystical ritual.
exampleUsage: The legend of the Golem of Prague has inspired countless books and films.
etymology: From Yiddish goylem, from Hebrew golem, meaning a shapeless mass or embryo.

definition: A belch or burp.
exampleUsage: He let out a greps at the dinner table and his mother gave him a look.
etymology: From Yiddish greps, related to German rülpsen, meaning to burp.

definition: A strong, tough person; sometimes used to describe a bully or enforcer.
exampleUsage: They sent a couple of shtarkers to collect the debt.
etymology: From Yiddish shtarker, from shtark (strong), from German stark.

definition: Messed up; bungled; ruined through excessive fiddling or botched repairs.
exampleUsage: He tried to fix the sink himself and now the whole bathroom is farpotshket.
etymology: From Yiddish farpotshket, past participle of farpotshken, meaning to fuss with or botch.

definition: A bastard; in Jewish law, a child of a forbidden union. Also used as a grudging compliment for a clever rascal.
exampleUsage: That mamzer talked his way out of a speeding ticket again.
etymology: From Yiddish mamzer, from Hebrew mamzer, meaning one born of a prohibited relationship.

definition: Dirty, filthy, or grimy.
exampleUsage: Take off those shmutsik boots before you come inside!
etymology: From Yiddish shmutsik, from shmuts (dirt), related to German Schmutz.

definition: A little bit; a small amount.
exampleUsage: Give me just a bisl more soup — I'm almost full.
etymology: From Yiddish bisl, from German bisschen, meaning a little bit.

fakeDefinition: To rummage through old belongings looking for something specific.
tell: Sounds like it could be Germanic, but the -shin ending is not a natural Yiddish suffix. Real Yiddish verbs typically end in -n or -en.

fakeDefinition: Small, harmless lies told to avoid hurting someone's feelings.
tell: While 'shvindl' (swindle) is a real Yiddish root, the -lach suffix is being misapplied here. The word doesn't exist in this form.

fakeDefinition: The witty comeback you think of too late, after the moment has passed.
tell: This is an invented compound mimicking Germanic word-building. While 'klopp' (knock) and 'vitz' (joke) are real elements, this compound was never coined in Yiddish.

fakeDefinition: False respect shown only to gain a favor; insincere flattery.
tell: Mixes Yiddish-sounding elements with the Hebrew word 'kavod' (respect/honor). The 'pish-' prefix doesn't function this way in real Yiddish.

fakeDefinition: A person who pretends to be sick to get out of work or obligations.
tell: Shmendrik IS actually a real Yiddish name (a foolish character from an Avrom Goldfaden operetta), but the definition given here is fabricated. The real meaning is simply a fool or a nobody.
basedOn: Real Yiddish name with false definition

fakeDefinition: To pinch pennies obsessely; extreme frugality beyond reason.
tell: The 'tzv-' consonant cluster looks Yiddish but this specific verb doesn't exist. Real Yiddish for a miser would use 'karger' or 'knaker.'

fakeDefinition: A deeply held grudge that lasts across generations within a family.
tell: While 'farbissen' (embittered) is a real Yiddish word, this feminine-inflected form with the specific 'generational grudge' meaning is fabricated.
basedOn: Real adjective farbissen with invented nominal meaning

fakeDefinition: A flashy but meaningless word used to impress people.
tell: A made-up compound of 'glitz' (shine) and 'vort' (word). While both roots exist in Yiddish, this compound was never actually used.

fakeDefinition: A habitual gambler who can't resist any bet or wager.
tell: Built from 'shpiln' (to play) but the -kever suffix is not a real Yiddish word-forming element. The authentic Yiddish term would be 'kartyozhnik.'

fakeDefinition: Unbreakable; describing something or someone that cannot be defeated.
tell: Combines 'brekhn' (to break) with a '-loz' suffix mimicking German '-los' (without). While the pattern looks plausible, this adjective doesn't exist in Yiddish.

fakeDefinition: A warm, comforting embrace from a grandparent.
tell: Sounds like 'kreplach' (Yiddish dumplings) but with a fabricated '-ush' ending. The cozy definition is designed to feel emotionally right even though the word is fake.

fakeDefinition: Overdressed in a showy, attention-seeking way; trying too hard.
tell: Mimics the real Yiddish 'ongeshtopt' (stuffed/packed) but changes the vowel and assigns a fashion-related meaning. The real word refers to being stuffed with food or crowded.
basedOn: Real word ongeshtopt with altered spelling and meaning

fakeDefinition: To sob uncontrollably while simultaneously trying to tell a story.
tell: The 'kv-' opening mimics real Yiddish words like kvetch and kvell, but the '-altzen' ending is invented. No such verb exists.

fakeDefinition: A face that reveals every emotion; an inability to hide feelings.
tell: Uses 'punim' (face), which is real Yiddish, combined with the fabricated prefix 'metzl-' to sound authentic. The combination doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real word punim with fake prefix

fakeDefinition: A scatterbrained person who can never remember where they put things.
tell: Combines 'dreck' (trash/dirt) and 'kopf' (head) in a way that looks plausible but this compound doesn't exist in Yiddish. A real Yiddish insult might use 'dreykopf' (someone who twists things).
basedOn: Similar to real word dreykopf

fakeDefinition: A person who eats loudly and without manners; a noisy eater.
tell: While 'fressn' (to eat like an animal) is real Yiddish, adding the Slavic '-nik' suffix this way is not standard. The word 'fresser' exists but 'fressnik' does not.
basedOn: Real verb fressn with wrong suffix

fakeDefinition: Dizzy or lightheaded from standing up too fast.
tell: Mimics the German-influenced adjective pattern (-ig ending) but 'schvund' is not a Yiddish root. The real Yiddish for dizzy would be 'shvindldik.'

fakeDefinition: Chaotic and noisy in an endearing way, like a bustling family gathering.
tell: While 'tuml' (commotion) is a real Yiddish root, the adjective form 'tumldik' with this specific warm connotation is fabricated. The real adjective is 'tumldig' meaning tumultuous.
basedOn: Real root tuml with slightly wrong form

fakeDefinition: To gossip maliciously while pretending to express concern.
tell: The word mimics Yiddish phonetics but 'platchken' is entirely fabricated. Real Yiddish for gossip includes 'rechiles' or 'shmuesn.'

fakeDefinition: A boorish person who ruins social gatherings with their crude behavior.
tell: While 'zhlub' (an uncouth person) is real Yiddish slang, the '-nik' suffix addition is redundant and not standard. 'Zhlub' already means what this word claims.
basedOn: Real word zhlub with unnecessary suffix

fakeDefinition: A small, insignificant gift given out of obligation rather than affection.
tell: Combines a vaguely Yiddish-sounding 'shmotz' with the diminutive '-kel' suffix. While '-l' and '-ele' are real Yiddish diminutives, '-kel' is not a standard Yiddish ending.
basedOn: Mimics shm- prefix and diminutive patterns

fakeDefinition: To boast about one's children's accomplishments to other parents.
tell: Uses the authentic 'kv-' opening found in kvetch and kvell, but '-antzen' is a fabricated verb ending. No such verb exists in Yiddish.

fakeDefinition: Emotionally shrunken; feeling small after being publicly humiliated.
tell: Mimics the Yiddish past participle form with 'ge-' prefix and '-en' ending. While 'getrunken' (drunk) exists, 'geshtrunken' with this meaning is fabricated.
basedOn: Mimics ge- past participle form like getrunken

fakeDefinition: A cozy, warm corner of the house where one feels safest.
tell: Uses the authentic '-leh' diminutive ending and a 'ts-' opening common in Yiddish, but 'tsimme-' is not a real root. Designed to evoke 'tsimmes' (a sweet stew) and warmth.
basedOn: Evokes tsimmes with real diminutive suffix

fakeDefinition: The quality of being perpetually slow and burdensome; chronic laziness.
tell: Combines the real verb 'shlepn' (to drag) with the abstract noun suffix '-keit.' While '-keit' is a real Yiddish suffix, it attaches to adjectives, not verb stems.
basedOn: Real root shlepn with misapplied -keit suffix

fakeDefinition: A child who hums constantly, even at inappropriate times.
tell: The '-chik' suffix doesn't exist in Yiddish. The word mimics Slavic-influenced diminutives but applies them incorrectly.

fakeDefinition: Hopelessly mixed up; confused beyond recovery after receiving contradictory advice.
tell: Very close to the real Yiddish 'farmisht' which means mixed up or confused — but the specific definition about contradictory advice is embellished. The word is borderline real, making it a tricky near-miss.
basedOn: Near-duplicate of real word farmisht with inflated definition

fakeDefinition: Leftover scraps of food too small to save but too good to throw away.
tell: Uses the authentic '-lach' plural suffix and Yiddish-sounding consonant cluster 'shtr-', but 'shtrunk' is not a Yiddish root.

fakeDefinition: A professional complainer; someone who has elevated whining to an art form.
tell: While 'kvetch' is a real Yiddish verb meaning to complain, the '-nik' suffix is redundant here since 'kvetcher' already exists as the agent noun.
basedOn: Real verb kvetch with redundant -nik suffix

fakeDefinition: Tears shed from worry rather than sadness; anxious crying.
tell: Compounds 'zorg' (worry, which is a real Yiddish word) with 'vasser' (water). While both roots are genuine, this compound was never coined in Yiddish.
basedOn: Real roots zorg + vasser in invented compound

fakeDefinition: Wholeness; the state of feeling complete and at peace after resolving a conflict.
tell: Derives from 'gonts' (whole/entire), a real Yiddish word, with the '-keit' abstract suffix. While the construction looks grammatically plausible, this specific abstract noun was never established in Yiddish.
basedOn: Real adjective gonts with plausible but unused -keit derivation

fakeDefinition: A person who takes everything literally; someone who misses all sarcasm and irony.
tell: Compounds 'plets' (a made-up element suggesting flatness) with 'kopf' (head). While '-kopf' compounds exist in Yiddish, 'plets-' is not a real Yiddish root used this way.

fakeDefinition: Physically tense from suppressing strong emotions; clenched up with unspoken anger.
tell: Uses the authentic 'far-' prefix with 'krampf' (cramp, from German). The form looks very plausible but this specific Yiddish word does not exist.
basedOn: Real far- prefix pattern with German-origin root

fakeDefinition: An embarrassing slip of the tongue that accidentally reveals a secret.
tell: Combines elements vaguely reminiscent of 'shmues' (chat) and 'shlimazl' (unlucky person) with an invented '-pas' suffix.

fakeDefinition: Cleverly disguised; said of someone pretending to be less intelligent than they are.
tell: Compounds 'klug' (smart, a real Yiddish word) with 'farshtelt' (disguised). While both elements are genuine, this specific compound adjective does not exist in Yiddish usage.
basedOn: Two real Yiddish words combined in an unused compound

fakeDefinition: A witty retort you think of only while walking down the stairs after leaving.
tell: A Yiddish-ified version of the French concept 'l'esprit de l'escalier.' Uses 'trepp' (stairs) and 'vertel' (little word), but this compound was never actually coined in Yiddish.
basedOn: Borrows concept from French with real Yiddish roots

fakeDefinition: A rude person who never learned basic manners; an oaf at the dinner table.
tell: Sounds like 'grubyaner' which is a real Yiddish word for a rude or coarse person. However, the '-ik' suffix replaces the correct '-er' ending.
basedOn: Near-miss of real word grubiyaner with wrong suffix

fakeDefinition: A steam room or sauna; by extension, any unbearably hot room.
tell: Compounds 'shvitz' (sweat, a real Yiddish word) with 'kasten' (box, from German). While 'shvitz' is authentic, 'shvitzkasten' was never used.
basedOn: Real root shvitz with German compound element

fakeDefinition: A decorative embroidered tablecloth used only for holiday meals.
tell: The '-ishl' ending is not a productive Yiddish suffix. The word is designed to sound like it could relate to 'terkish' (Turkish).

fakeDefinition: Excessively patched or repaired; something fixed so many times it's worse than before.
tell: Very close to the real Yiddish 'ongepotchket' (excessively overdone, cluttered). The 'un-' prefix changes the meaning subtly but this specific form does not exist. The real word uses 'on-' not 'un-'.
basedOn: Near-miss of real word ongepotchket with wrong prefix

fakeDefinition: A sharp, cutting remark delivered with perfect timing; a verbal jab.
tell: Compounds 'shprits' (spray/squirt) with 'vort' (word). Both roots exist in Yiddish, but this compound was never formed.
basedOn: Two real roots shprits + vort in unused compound

fakeDefinition: A pitifully small portion of food; a serving so meager it evokes sympathy.
tell: Based on 'nebbish/nebekh' (a pitiable person), with the diminutive '-el' added. While 'nebekh' is real, converting it to describe a food portion is an invented usage.
basedOn: Real word nebekh with creative diminutive

fakeDefinition: Nonsensical spinning arguments that go in circles and never reach a conclusion.
tell: Evokes 'dreydl' (spinning top) and uses the 'far-' prefix with '-lach' plural. While 'fardreyenish' (something twisted) exists, this specific form is invented.
basedOn: Evokes dreydl and real prefix far- with -lach plural

fakeDefinition: A very small, insignificant town; the kind of place no one has ever heard of.
tell: Combines 'shmekel' (a small/trivial thing) with 'dorf' (village). This whimsical compound mimics Germanic place-name patterns but was never an actual Yiddish word.
basedOn: Germanic compound pattern with Yiddish roots

fakeDefinition: The specific ache of watching your child make the same mistakes you did.
tell: A literal calque of 'brokenhearted' using Yiddish-sounding elements 'brokh' (break) and 'hart' (heart). Yiddish does not form this compound.

fakeDefinition: A clever trick involving misdirection; a sleight of hand in negotiation.
tell: Combines 'knippel' (a club or stick, loosely) with 'shtik' (piece/routine). While 'shtik' is well-known, the compound is fabricated.

fakeDefinition: Thoroughly exhausted from emotional rather than physical labor; drained from caring too much.
tell: Uses the real 'oys-' prefix (meaning out/thoroughly) and 'ge-' past participle marker, but 'mutchit' is borrowed from Slavic 'muchit' (to torment) in a form that doesn't occur in standard Yiddish.
basedOn: Real oys- prefix with non-standard Slavic borrowing

fakeDefinition: A perfectionist who insists on doing everything themselves rather than accepting help.
tell: Compounds 'gants' (whole/entire) with 'macher' (a doer, big shot). Both are real Yiddish words, but this specific compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real words gants + macher in unused compound

fakeDefinition: A daydreamer; someone who always seems half-asleep and lost in thought.
tell: Combines 'shlufn' (to sleep, a real Yiddish verb) with 'kopf' (head). The compound follows Germanic word-building logic but was never coined in Yiddish.
basedOn: Real verb shlufn + kopf compound

fakeDefinition: Having wandered aimlessly for so long you've forgotten your original destination.
tell: Uses the productive 'far-' prefix with a Yiddish-sounding but non-existent root 'valgert.' The real Yiddish for wandering uses 'vander' or 'blondjen.'
basedOn: Real far- prefix with invented root

fakeDefinition: The last spoonful of soup scraped from the bottom of the bowl.
tell: Uses 'zup' (suggesting 'zup/sup' for soup) with the '-chik' suffix which is Russian, not Yiddish.

fakeDefinition: A friend's house where you feel so comfortable you treat it like your own home.
tell: Compounds 'khaver' (friend, a real Yiddish word) with 'shtub' (room/house, also real). The compound looks plausible but was never established in Yiddish.
basedOn: Two real words khaver + shtub combined

fakeDefinition: Someone who lets secrets slip accidentally; a person with a leaky mouth.
tell: Combines 'dropl' (drop, not a standard Yiddish form) with 'moyl' (mouth, a real Yiddish word). The concept is creative but 'dropl' is an anglicized form.
basedOn: Real word moyl with anglicized partner

fakeDefinition: A person who enjoys being sick because of the attention and sympathy it brings.
tell: Uses 'krenk' (illness, a real Yiddish word) with the invented element '-varg.' '-varg' is not a Yiddish suffix or word-forming element.
basedOn: Real root krenk with fabricated suffix

fakeDefinition: Dressed to the nines for an occasion that turns out to be casual; embarrassingly overdressed.
tell: Uses the authentic 'oys-ge-' prefix combination but 'strasselt' is not a real Yiddish root.

fakeDefinition: A troublemaker who stirs up drama in everyone's gut; someone who causes collective anxiety.
tell: Combines 'kishke' (intestine/gut, real Yiddish) with 'macher' (doer/big shot, also real). The metaphorical definition is creative but this compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real words kishke + macher in invented compound

fakeDefinition: The quality of destiny; the feeling that something was always meant to happen.
tell: Derives from 'beshert' (destiny/fated, a real Yiddish word) with '-keit' suffix. While 'beshert' is authentic and '-keit' is a real suffix, this abstract noun form was never established.
basedOn: Real word beshert with plausible but non-existent -keit derivation

fakeDefinition: Clumsy, ineffective fiddling with something; making repairs that only make things worse.
tell: Based loosely on 'patsh' (a slap) and 'patshken' (to mess around), but the '-nyu' ending is not a Yiddish suffix.
basedOn: Based on patsh root with non-Yiddish suffix

fakeDefinition: An obsessive, nagging worry that you cannot shake no matter how irrational it is.
tell: A three-element compound using 'ayn' (in), 'gehakt' (chopped), and 'zorg' (worry). Each element is real Yiddish but this triple compound was never formed.
basedOn: Three real Yiddish elements in an overly complex compound

fakeDefinition: Nonsensical chatter that sounds important but means absolutely nothing.
tell: The root 'shmond' doesn't exist in Yiddish, though it sounds like it could be related to 'shmontses' (nonsense). The '-erish' suffix mimics Yiddish adjective patterns but is applied to a nonexistent root.
basedOn: Invented root resembling shmontses with -erish suffix

fakeDefinition: A tiny, insignificant amount; a quantity too small to matter.
tell: The 'kv-' opening mimics kvetch/kvell, and '-chik' resembles Slavic diminutives, but this word doesn't exist.
basedOn: kv- prefix pattern combined with Slavic diminutive

fakeDefinition: A loud, wet kiss planted on someone's cheek by a relative.
tell: While 'shmats' sounds like it could relate to 'schmuck' or 'schmaltz,' the '-erei' suffix is borrowed from German but never combined with this fake root.
basedOn: German -erei suffix with invented root

fakeDefinition: A topsy-turvy situation where nothing makes sense; total disorder.
tell: Combines 'drey' (to turn/twist) and 'velt' (world) — both real Yiddish words — but this compound was never actually formed.
basedOn: Real roots drey + velt combined incorrectly

fakeDefinition: Tipsy or slightly drunk; pleasantly buzzed after a few drinks.
tell: The 'far-' prefix is a genuine Yiddish verbal prefix, and 'shnok' sounds plausible, but this word is entirely invented. The real Yiddish for drunk is 'shiker.'
basedOn: Far- prefix pattern with invented root

fakeDefinition: A loudmouth who dominates every conversation at a gathering.
tell: The '-nik' suffix is legitimate Yiddish, but 'bulvan' is a Russian word (meaning blockhead) that never entered standard Yiddish.
basedOn: Russian root with Yiddish -nik suffix

fakeDefinition: A person with fuzzy, muddled thinking; someone whose thoughts are all tangled up.
tell: Combines 'kneydl' (dumpling) and 'kopf' (head) — both real Yiddish words — but this compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real roots kneydl + kopf in fake compound

fakeDefinition: A person who always carries too many bags and packages wherever they go.
tell: Double-compounds two real Yiddish verbs: 'trogn' (to carry) and 'shlepn' (to drag). While each root is authentic, combining them into one word is not standard.
basedOn: Two real verbs trogn + shlepn combined artificially

fakeDefinition: A woman who gives unsolicited advice as if she were your mother.
tell: This is essentially a German compound given a Yiddish-sounding definition. Real Yiddish wouldn't compound these words this way.
basedOn: German-style compound passed off as Yiddish

fakeDefinition: A tiny smear or spot of food on your clothing that you don't notice until someone points it out.
tell: Diminutive-sounding blend of 'shmirn' (to smear) with an invented '-tzl' ending. '-tzl' is not a standard Yiddish suffix.
basedOn: Shmirn root with fake diminutive suffix

fakeDefinition: The particular pride one feels when a child succeeds beyond all expectations.
tell: Smashes two real Yiddish words together: 'kvell' (to beam with pride) and 'naches' (joy from children). Both exist independently, but this compound was never formed.
basedOn: Real words kvell + naches merged into fake compound

fakeDefinition: A bathhouse regular who treats the steam room as their personal therapy office.
tell: Combines 'shvitsn' (to sweat) and 'bodl' (a fabricated diminutive of 'bod'/bath). The '-l' diminutive and the specific meaning are invented.
basedOn: Shvitsn root with Germanic bath reference

fakeDefinition: The quality of being nothing special; deliberate ordinariness.
tell: Built from 'nisht' (nothing) with the abstract noun suffix '-keit.' While both elements are real Yiddish, the combination doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real word nisht with -keit abstraction suffix

fakeDefinition: Deliberate rudeness disguised as honesty; brutish frankness.
tell: While 'grub' (coarse) is real Yiddish, the '-yanstvo' ending is a Slavic abstract noun suffix that doesn't appear in standard Yiddish.
basedOn: Real adjective grub with Russian abstract suffix

fakeDefinition: Completely drained of energy; tapped out emotionally and physically.
tell: Uses the real Yiddish prefix 'oys-' but attaches it to 'tzapt,' which sounds like English 'tapped/zapped' given a Yiddish phonetic disguise.
basedOn: Real prefix oys- with anglicized verb root

fakeDefinition: A habitual troublemaker who causes problems through sheer incompetence rather than malice.
tell: Derives from the real Yiddish slang 'shmegege' (a fool) but adds the '-nik' suffix unnecessarily. 'Shmegege' already functions as a complete word.
basedOn: Real slang shmegege with redundant -nik suffix

fakeDefinition: To exaggerate an illness for sympathy; to play up being sick.
tell: Built from 'krank' (sick) but altered to 'kronk' and given a fabricated verb form.
basedOn: Altered form of krank/krenk with invented verb pattern

fakeDefinition: A web of small lies that has grown so complex the liar can no longer keep track.
tell: Uses the real prefix 'far-' and a root resembling 'lign' (to lie), but the '-enish' suffix is not standard Yiddish.
basedOn: Far- prefix with lign root and wrong abstract suffix

fakeDefinition: A person who appears clean and proper but secretly lives in filth.
tell: Combines 'shmutz' (dirt) with 'finkel' (a common Yiddish surname element meaning 'sparkle'). While both elements are authentic, this contradictory compound was never coined.
basedOn: Real word shmutz + name element finkel

fakeDefinition: Puffed up with self-importance; acting superior without justification.
tell: This closely mimics the real Yiddish word 'ongeblozen,' which actually does mean 'sulky' or 'pouting' (literally 'blown up'). The definition given here — about arrogance rather than sulking — is the fabrication.
basedOn: Real word ongeblozen with wrong definition

fakeDefinition: A face permanently set in an expression of dissatisfaction; resting complaint face.
tell: Combines 'kvetch' (to complain) and 'punim' (face) — both real Yiddish words — but this compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real words kvetch + punim combined

fakeDefinition: A person who takes long, aimless walks to avoid dealing with their problems.
tell: While 'shpatsirn' (to stroll) is a real Yiddish verb, adding '-nik' to create an agent noun this way is not standard. A walker would be 'shpatsirganger.'
basedOn: Real verb shpatsirn with wrong agent suffix

fakeDefinition: A small, cozy room where the family gathers for warmth and conversation.
tell: Sounds like it could relate to 'tsimmer' (room, from German 'Zimmer') with a diminutive '-el' ending, but the word is fabricated.
basedOn: German Zimmer with Yiddish diminutive pattern

fakeDefinition: Two-hearted; the feeling of being torn between two equally strong loyalties.
tell: Combines 'tsvey' (two) and 'harts' (heart) with the adjective suffix '-ik.' This compound was never formed in Yiddish.
basedOn: Real words tsvey + harts with adjective suffix

fakeDefinition: A pit of sentimentality; a movie, song, or story that is excessively emotional.
tell: Combines 'shmaltz' (rendered fat/sentimentality) with 'grub' (pit/coarse), but this compound doesn't exist in Yiddish.
basedOn: Real words shmaltz + grub in invented compound

fakeDefinition: An underlying hint or subtle suggestion that something is not quite right.
tell: Uses the real prefix 'unter-' (under) and a root resembling 'shmekn' (to smell/taste), but this nominalized form doesn't exist.
basedOn: Unter- prefix with shmekn verb root

fakeDefinition: An elaborate prank planned and executed by a group of friends.
tell: Merges 'kibitz' (to offer unwanted advice) and 'shtik' (piece/routine) — both real Yiddish words — but this compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real words kibitz + shtik merged

fakeDefinition: A person who takes over hosting duties at someone else's party without being asked.
tell: Based on the real word 'balebuste' (a capable homemaker) but with an invented '-nik' suffix and altered meaning.
basedOn: Real word balebuste with wrong suffix and shifted meaning

fakeDefinition: The shameless spirit of asking for freebies; the audacity of a chronic moocher.
tell: Combines 'schnorr' (to beg/mooch) with 'geist' (spirit/ghost, from German). While 'schnorrer' is a real Yiddish word, this abstract '-geist' compound follows German patterns.
basedOn: Real root schnorr with German -geist compound style

fakeDefinition: A small amount of money kept hidden for emergencies; secret savings.
tell: Combines 'shtikl' (a little piece) and 'gelt' (money) — both real Yiddish words — but this specific compound doesn't exist. The real Yiddish for secret money would use 'knipl.'
basedOn: Real words shtikl + gelt in plausible but nonexistent compound

fakeDefinition: Pleasantly time-wasting; describing an activity that is unproductive but deeply enjoyable.
tell: Uses the real prefix 'far-' and a root resembling 'vayln' (to linger) with the adjective suffix '-dik,' but this exact adjective was never formed.
basedOn: Far- prefix with vayl root and -dik adjective suffix

fakeDefinition: Reluctant pride; being proud of someone while pretending not to care.
tell: Combines 'shver' (difficult/father-in-law) with 'shtoltz' (pride). Both are real Yiddish words, but this compound doesn't exist.
basedOn: Real words shver + shtoltz combined

fakeDefinition: A daydreamer's sanctuary; an imaginary place of comfort one retreats to mentally.
tell: Compounds 'kholem' (dream) and 'shtub' (house/room) — both authentic Yiddish — but this compound was never formed.
basedOn: Real words kholem + shtub in invented compound

fakeDefinition: The feeling of deep contentment from having exactly enough; satisfaction with sufficiency.
tell: Built from 'genug' (enough) with the German abstract suffix '-schaft.' This follows German, not Yiddish, word formation.
basedOn: Real word genug with German abstract suffix

fakeDefinition: Charmingly mixed up; endearingly confused in a way that makes others smile.
tell: Based on the real Yiddish 'farmisht' (mixed up/confused) but adds a fabricated diminutive-ish '-elekh' ending.
basedOn: Real word farmisht with invented affectionate suffix
